data.title: Microsoft fades to black as it removes 'blue screen of death' from Windows
data.description:
Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) plans to replace its notorious blue screen of death from Windows and replace it with a black one as the tech giant retools its security features following last summer's CrowdStrike (NASDAQ:CRWD) debacle.

Last July, a worldwide outage led to the blue screen of death on Windows systems, while users worldwide were unable to access Microsoft 365 services. The outage was caused by a glitch in an update of CrowdStrike's cybersecurity platform Falcon Sensor. The outage hit backend operations at multiple industry sectors, including airlines, banks, hospitals and broadcast media, across the world.

As a result, Microsoft has launched the Windows Resiliency Initiative, or WRI. 

"WRI is an initiative designed to make all digital environments touched by Microsoft products more secure and resilient," the company said in a blog post. "WRI prioritizes preventing, managing and recovering from security and reliability incidents, mitigating issues swiftly and providing seamless recovery across the Windows platform."

One of the changes includes a faster recovery when Windows experiences unexpected restarts. For more than 40 years, this was accompanied with a blue screen and an error message. The new unexpected restart screen will now be black.

"The updated UI (user interface) improves readability and aligns better with Windows 11 design principles, while preserving the technical information on the screen for when it is needed," Microsoft said.

It will be released for all editions of Windows 11, version 24H2 devices later this summer. It also includes a better remedy for multiple unexpected restarts.

"In the case of consecutive unexpected restarts, devices can get stuck in the Windows Recovery Environment, impacting productivity and often requiring IT teams to spend significant time troubleshooting and restoring affected devices," the company said. "This is where quick machine recovery can help. When a widespread outage affects devices from starting properly, Microsoft can broadly deploy targeted remediations to affected devices via Windows RE—automating fixes with QMR and quickly getting users to a productive state without requiring complex manual intervention from IT."

Windows is also improving its endpoint security platform and its virus detection capabilities through the Microsoft Virus Initiative, where it works with cybersecurity partners such as CrowdStrike (NASDAQ:CRWD) and SentinelOne (S).

"Security product updates must be gradual, leverage deployment rings and leverage monitoring to minimize negative impacts," Microsoft said.

data.title: Uber is downgraded by Canaccord in a contrarian call
data.description:
Uber Technologies (NYSE:UBER) fell in early trading on Friday after Canaccord downgraded the mobility transportation stock to a Hold rating from Buy.

While Uber (NYSE:UBER) was noted to be strong on the ground operations and other tactical elements, analyst Michael Graham warned that the future is unclear as an alternative scenario is that the new world is dominated by a few AV behemoths that control the value chain and leave both Uber (NYSE:UBER) and Lyft (LYFT) reflecting on the golden days of the past. "It is truly unclear," he summarized about the path ahead for the San Francisco-based company.

Wall Street analysts have a consensus Buy rating on Uber (UBER), with 44 Buy-equivalents ratings on the books vs. 10 Hold-equivalent ratings and no Sell-equivalent ratings. Seeking Alpha analysts also have a consensus Buy rating on Uber (UBER).

Shares of Uber (UBER) were down 1.7% in Friday morning action to $91.60. The 52-week high for the stock is $94.38.

data.title: Consumer sentiment ticks higher as inflation expectations cool again
data.description: The U.S. consumer sentiment gauge came in at 60.7 in June's final estimate, compared with the mid-month reading of 60.5 and May's 52.2, according to data released by the University of Michigan on Friday.

data.title: Weyerhaeuser downwardly revises Q2 guidance for Wood Products unit
data.description:
Timber REIT Weyerhaeuser (NYSE:WY) has revised downward its Q2 earnings and adjusted EBITDA guidance for the Wood Products segment.

In a June 26 investor presentation, Weyerhaeuser said it now expects Wood Products earnings and adjusted EBITDA to be ~$20M lower than the Q1 level. The previous guidance was "slightly higher than Q1".

The company attributed the revision to a softer demand environment, slightly lower sales realizations from Q1, and slightly lower results than Q1 in the segment.

Weyerhaeuser maintained its outlook for the Real Estate, Energy & Natural Resources and Timberlands segments.

Shares were +0.72% Friday pre-market to $26.51.

data.title: Ascent Solar Technologies signs supply contract with US-based defense solutions provider
data.description:
• Ascent Solar Technologies (NASDAQ:ASTI) has entered into a teaming agreement to supply a US-based defense solutions provider with its technology solutions for upcoming orbital applications and future missions.
  • ASTI -6.34% premarket to $2.66.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Bone Biologics prices $5M public offering with warrants; shares up 45%
data.description:
• Bone Biologics (NASDAQ:BBLG) priced a public offering of 1.25M shares (or equivalents) at $4/share.
  • The offering includes Series D and Series E warrants to purchase up to 1.25M shares each.
  • Series D warrants are immediately exercisable at $4/share and expire in 5 years.
  • Series E short-term warrants are also immediately exercisable at $4/share and expire in 18 months.
  • Gross proceeds from the offering are expected to be ~$5M.
  • The closing of the offering is anticipated around June 30, 2025.
  • BBLG shares up 45% premarket on Friday.
  • Source: Press release

data.title: Shell hikes natural gas production at Norway’s Ormen Lange field
data.description:
Shell (NYSE:SHEL) and its partners said Friday have started up two subsea compression stations at the Ormen Lange natural gas field in the Norwegian Sea to help boost recovery of gas reserves, which are exported to European markets, Reuters reported.

Shell (NYSE:SHEL) said the compression stations will raise the gas recovery rate to 85% from the current 75%, adding an additional 30B-50B cm of gas.

Ormen Lange's gas, processed at Nyhamna on the west coast of Norway, is being exported to the U.K. and the European Union.

Ormen Lange is operated by Shell (SHEL), which holds a 17.8% stake in the field, with the other stakes held by Petoro at 36.5%, Equinor (EQNR) with 25.3%, Orlen Upstream Norway at 14%, and Vaar Energi with 6.3%.

Norway has been increasing its gas production since 2022 when it overtook Russia as Europe's top gas supplier.

data.title: The U.S. isn't seeking to 'decouple' from China but to 'de-risk', Bessent says
data.description:
"We don't want to decouple from China," Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said on Friday during a media interview. He spoke after President Donald Trump announced a trade deal with China on Thursday, though the president provided few details of the deal.

Still, the U.S. needs to de-risk, he said. "China counts for 30% of manufacturing in the world, and that is not sustainable. They need to consume more," he said. "Over the next 18 to 24 months, we will de-risk and become self-sufficient," he said in an interview on Fox Business's "Mornings with Maria."

The agreement will increase rare-earth magnets flowing into the U.S. from China. "i am confident now, we, as agreed, the magnets will flow," Bessent said. Under the agreement, the U.S. has put in counter-measures against the Chinese.

"What we're seeing here is a de-escalation under President Trump's leadership," he said.

The U.S. still has a 30% tariff on Chinese goods, and China still has a 10% tariff on U.S. goods, he said. This is a centerpiece of fair trade, which the U.S. hasn't seen in 40 years, he added.

Asked whether the U.S. can trust any deal that China signs, Bessent responded with President Ronald Reagan's oft-repeated phrase of "trust, but verify."

Bessent expects trade deals with more countries will be struck in coming weeks. "A lot of countries are feeling pressure. We have countries approaching us with very good deals," he said. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick expects 10 more deals soon, he added. "I think we can have trade wrapped up by Labor Day," Bessent said.

On Thursday, though, Bloomberg reported that trade negotiations had stalled with partners like Japan, India, and the European Union.

The Treasury Secretary also spoke about an agreement he reached with G-7 allies to remove Section 899, what's known as the "revenge tax," from the proposed tax legislation. Section 899 was a potential weapon to push back on unfair taxes from other countries, he said. "Our tax sovereignty is now guaranteed." He, however, explained that Section 891, which has been on the book for years, allows the president to impose taxes to counter unfair taxes from other nations.

One of the sticking points in the Republican's tax bill is the state and local tax ("SALT") deduction cap (currently at $10K, but due to expire under the current code. "We are very close to a deal" that's going to help voters in the SALT Republicans' districts, but also fair for the American people, Bessent said.

Senate Republicans had hoped to vote on the "One Big, Beautiful Bill" on Thursday. But that timeline stretched out after the Senate parliamentarian struck down several key parts of the bill, saying they didn't meet the rules for inclusion in a reconciliation bill, which only requires a simple majority to pass instead of a 60-vote supermajority. Congress still aims to get a bill on the president's desk by July 4.

Even though Q1 GDP was revised down on Thursday, Bessent said, "I think we'll see substantial acceleration" in the second half of the year, given the three pillars of Trump's agenda — trade, taxes and deregulation.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Fed's Kashkari expects two 2025 rate cuts, first possibly in September
data.description:
Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis President Kneel Kashkari sees room for two interest-rate cuts this year, while warning the central bank should not lock into a monetary easing path as tariff effects could hit inflation in the future, according to his essay published on Friday.

His rate projections for 2025 has not changed since December, when he "was unsure if the underlying disinflationary dynamics that we saw in 2024 would continue this year." Since then, he added, economic uncertainty surged in the face of President Donald Trump's sweeping tariffs, although inflation data continued to reflect progress toward the Fed's 2% goal.

"These opposing signals have led me to maintain my outlook for two cuts over the remainder of 2025, implying a possible first cut in September, barring some surprising development before then," the essay, published on the Minneapolis Fed's website, said.

Excluding Fed governors Christopher Waller and Michelle Bowman -- who have signaled a rate reduction as soon as July -- the majority of officials who spoke this week made plain that uncertainty remains high and a July move is not being seriously considered.

“While we gather more evidence on the true tariff shock affecting the economy, I believe we should put more emphasis on the actual inflation and real economic data that we are seeing without committing to an easing policy path in case the effects of tariffs are merely delayed,” Kashkari wrote.

If there are no trade deals and levies stay high, companies might have to pass those tariff costs on to consumers, Kashkari noted, citing his discussions with business leaders.
